painting</a>\n          <a href=\"#pigments\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Pigments</a>\n          <a href=\"#special-brushes\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Water, lift, rainbow</a>\n          <a href=\"#mask\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Masking fluid</a>\n\n          <a href=\"#composition\" class=\"docs-nav-section\">Composition layers</a>\n          <a href=\"#backgrounds\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Backgrounds</a>\n          <a href=\"#animations\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Animations</a>\n          <a href=\"#visualizations\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Visualizations</a>\n          <a href=\"#text\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Text painting</a>\n          <a href=\"#svg\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">SVG tracing</a>\n\n          <a href=\"#controls\" class=\"docs-nav-section\">Canvas controls</a>\n          <a href=\"#paper-wetness\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Paper wetness</a>\n          <a href=\"#toggles\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Toggles</a>\n          <a href=\"#actions\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Actions</a>\n          <a href=\"#performance\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Performance</a>\n\n          <a href=\"#api\" class=\"docs-nav-section\">Programmatic API</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-quickstart\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Quickstart</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-cursors\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Cursors</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-modes\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Modes</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-text-svg\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Text &amp; SVG</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-toggles\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Toggles</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-actions\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Actions</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-perf\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Performance</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-coords\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Coordinates</a>\n          <a href=\"#api-state\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">State snapshot</a>\n\n          <a href=\"#concepts\" class=\"docs-nav-section\">Concepts</a>\n          <a href=\"#concept-physics\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">The physics</a>\n          <a href=\"#concept-active-rect\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Active rect</a>\n          <a href=\"#concept-mask-rect\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">Mask rect</a>\n          <a href=\"#concept-webgl\" class=\"docs-nav-item\">WebGL render path</a>\n\n          <a href=\"#references\" class=\"docs-nav-section\">References</a>\n        </nav>\n      </aside>\n\n      <main class=\"docs-main\">\n\n      <!-- ============================== INTRODUCTION ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\">\n        <h1 id=\"intro\">Watercolor</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          A browser-based interactive watercolor simulation. Paints with\n          three real pigments through a physically-motivated wet-paper\n          model \u2014 capillary diffusion, edge darkening, Kubelka-Munk\n          color mixing, granulation, masking fluid. Composable layers,\n          a complete programmatic API, optional GPU acceleration.\n          Single HTML file, no dependencies, runs offline.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"getting-started\">Getting started</h2>\n        <p>\n          Move your pointer over the canvas above to see the brush\n          cursor. Click and drag to paint. The dab spreads through the\n          wet substrate, bleeds at its edges, and mixes with adjacent\n          color via Kubelka-Munk reflectance \u2014 the same equations\n          published in the seminal SIGGRAPH 1997 paper by Curtis,\n          Anderson, Seims, Fleischer, and Salesin.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The control panel at the bottom contains everything: brush\n          and pigment selection, background washes, autonomous\n          animations, generative visualizations, text and SVG painters,\n          paper-wetness presets, fade-painting, masking fluid, and the\n          WebGL render toggle. Hover any control for an inline tooltip\n          explaining what it does.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"philosophy\">Design philosophy</h2>\n        <p>\n          Every feature composes through one path: <code>paintAt</code>.\n          When you click the canvas, when the Tornado animation paints\n          its funnel, when the Kaleidoscope visualization stamps its\n          rotational pattern, when text or SVG geometry traces \u2014 all\n          of them call the same function and inherit the same physics.\n          That's why every brush color works with every painter:\n          Rainbow text, Mask-traced SVG, Water-brush dot-burst.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The simulation is honest about being a model. Pigment is\n          three pigments. Paper is one cream substrate with subtle\n          warm noise. Time is sim-step ticks at the requested\n          resolution. No filters, no shortcuts, no post-processing.\n          What you see is the wet-paper equations playing out.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== BRUSHES ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\">\n        <h1 id=\"brushes\">Brushes &amp; painting</h1>\n\n        <h2 id=\"pigments\">Pigments</h2>\n        <p>\n          Three pigments from Figure 5 of the Curtis et al. paper,\n          chosen as a complementary triad for color-mixing demos:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Quinacridone Rose</dt>\n          <dd>Magenta-red. High staining (\u03c9=5.5) and granulation (\u03b3=0.81) \u2014 sticks where it lands, fills in paper texture.</dd>\n          <dt>Hansa Yellow</dt>\n          <dd>Warm yellow. Low staining (\u03c9=1.0) and granulation (\u03b3=0.08) \u2014 flows freely, blends smoothly.</dd>\n          <dt>Cerulean Blue</dt>\n          <dd>Cool blue. The paper's authentic Cerulean is a famous lifting pigment; we raise its staining and granulation here for visual consistency with Quinacridone Rose.</dd>\n        </dl>\n        <p>\n          Pigments mix via <strong>Kubelka-Munk reflectance</strong>,\n          not RGB averaging. Mixing yellow and blue gives a real\n          watercolor green \u2014 not the muddy gray you'd get from adding\n          their colors directly. The K and S values per channel\n          (absorption and scattering) determine how light propagates\n          through the layer.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"special-brushes\">Water, lift, rainbow</h2>\n        <p>Three special brushes alongside the pigments:</p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Water</dt>\n          <dd>Deposits water and pressure without pigment. Lifts a fraction of deposited pigment back into suspension at each touched cell, letting you re-mobilize dry paint for blending, edge-softening, or washout effects.</dd>\n          <dt>Lift</dt>\n          <dd>Removes pigment from each touched cell. Drag-compounding: a single dab at brush center keeps 78% of the pigment; ten dabs keep 8%. Leaves water and pressure alone so the surroundings don't bleed.</dd>\n          <dt>Rainbow</dt>\n          <dd>Same dynamics as a regular pigment brush, but the stamp color cycles through rose \u2192 yellow \u2192 blue over time. Long strokes traverse the spectrum.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"mask\">Masking fluid</h2>\n        <p>\n          The amber-yellow swatch at the end of the brush row. Paints a\n          translucent reservation layer over the paper:\n        </p>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>Paint mask first</strong> \u2014 masked cells reject all subsequent paint, water, and lift</li>\n          <li><strong>Paint over mask</strong> \u2014 pigment frozen beneath stays unchanged through every sim step</li>\n          <li><strong>Click \"Remove mask\"</strong> \u2014 the rubbery layer comes off in one pass, revealing whatever was frozen underneath (paper, or pigment that was there when you masked)</li>\n        </ul>\n        <p>\n          The visible amber tint scales with mask density \u2014 a single\n          dab is faint, dense coverage is fully amber. The tint sits\n          on top of any pigment underneath, so you see through the\n          mask to your painting while it's working. This is how real\n          masking fluid behaves on real paper.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== COMPOSITION ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\">\n        <h1 id=\"composition\">Composition layers</h1>\n        <p>\n          Three independent autonomous painters sit in the controls\n          row, each driven by its own dropdown. They paint <em>through\n          the same simulation your brush uses</em>, so wet-on-wet\n          mixing, edge darkening, and granulation all apply.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"backgrounds\">Backgrounds</h2>\n        <p>\n          One-shot washes painted across the canvas in ~0.75 seconds.\n          Five options:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Day</dt>     <dd>Pale cerulean sky wash, lighter toward the horizon.</dd>\n          <dt>Dawn</dt>    <dd>Cerulean top, rose mid, yellow horizon \u2014 gentle pre-sunrise gradient.</dd>\n          <dt>Sunset</dt>  <dd>Warm rose-and-yellow horizon over cool cerulean zenith.</dd>\n          <dt>Night</dt>   <dd>Deep blue-purple, denser than the others \u2014 rose tints the upper sky.</dd>\n          <dt>Tornado</dt> <dd>The sickly green-yellow tornado sky from American Plains storms. Pair with the Tornado animation for the full picture.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"animations\">Animations</h2>\n        <p>\n          Continuously-running weather and ambient scenes. Each calls\n          <code>paintAt</code> on its own schedule with appropriate\n          brushes:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>AI painter</dt>    <dd>Weighted stroke strategies that paint indefinitely with all three pigments.</dd>\n          <dt>Rainy</dt>         <dd>Falling drops of cerulean and water; rose flecks for variety.</dd>\n          <dt>Sunny</dt>         <dd>Slow horizontal warm sweeps across the canvas.</dd>\n          <dt>Windy</dt>         <dd>Fast horizontal streaks with periodic gust-direction flips.</dd>\n          <dt>Thunderstorm</dt>  <dd>Heavy rain plus periodic yellow lightning bolts.</dd>\n          <dt>Partly cloudy</dt> <dd>Three large slow clouds drifting across the sky.</dd>\n          <dt>Snowing</dt>       <dd>Cerulean wash + water-brush flakes lifting it for white snow trails.</dd>\n          <dt>Snowing (additive)</dt> <dd>Tiny cerulean dabs with rose flecks \u2014 the original snow preset, kept for the additive look.</dd>\n          <dt>Tornado</dt>       <dd>14 cursors orbit a meandering vertical axis with layered-sine motion. 5-second pause between passes. Pair with the Tornado background for the green sky.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"visualizations\">Visualizations</h2>\n        <p>\n          Generative loops that run indefinitely, showcasing what the\n          simulation can do:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Kaleidoscope</dt> <dd>Six cursors at 60\u00b0 rotational symmetry around canvas center. Phases cycle through arc-trace (organic curves), dot-burst (sharp hexagonal dots), and rose-petals (r = R\u00b7cos(k\u00b7\u03b8) flower curves).</dd>\n          <dt>Lissajous</dt>    <dd>A master point traces sin(a\u00b7t)\u00b7R<sub>x</sub>, sin(b\u00b7t)\u00b7R<sub>y</sub> where (a, b) morph slowly via low-frequency carrier sines. Dense overlapping curves that never repeat.</dd>\n          <dt>Flow field</dt>   <dd>22 particles follow a vector field derived from the paperH noise + a slow time offset. Wind-blown / topographic line patterns.</dd>\n          <dt>Pulse</dt>        <dd>Concentric rings expand from random epicenters. Strength tapers as each ring grows, giving fading wave-fronts that interfere where they overlap.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"text\">Text painting</h2>\n        <p>\n          Type any phrase in the Text control's input, press Enter or\n          click <strong>Paint</strong>. The text renders at 96px Georgia\n          bold to an offscreen canvas, then each ink pixel becomes a\n          <code>paintAt</code> call. Uses the currently-selected brush \u2014\n          try Rainbow for shifting hue, or Mask to reserve a text-shaped\n          area for a later wash.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"svg\">SVG tracing</h2>\n        <p>\n          Click <strong>Load SVG</strong> to pick a vector file. The\n          parser walks every <code>path</code>, <code>line</code>,\n          <code>polyline</code>, <code>polygon</code>, <code>rect</code>,\n          <code>circle</code>, and <code>ellipse</code> element,\n          converts each to a path-d string, and uses\n          <code>SVGPathElement.getPointAtLength</code> to sample\n          arc-length-parametrized points along it. Points are queued\n          and 10 are painted per frame, so the SVG draws itself with a\n          watercolor brush over a couple of seconds. Uses the\n          currently-selected pigment.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CONTROLS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\">\n        <h1 id=\"controls\">Canvas controls</h1>\n\n        <h2 id=\"paper-wetness\">Paper wetness</h2>\n        <p>\n          Five named presets borrowed from real watercolor practice,\n          tied to evaporation rate:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Flooded</dt>     <dd>Pooled water and dramatic blooms. ~9.5 second half-life. Least performant.</dd>\n          <dt>Wet-on-wet (default)</dt> <dd>Soft Curtis-style bleed. ~3.2 second half-life. Balanced for interactive use.</dd>\n          <dt>Damp</dt>        <dd>Controlled blending, firmer edges. ~1.2 second half-life.</dd>\n          <dt>Dry-brush</dt>   <dd>Paint sits where placed, granular texture shows. ~0.5 second half-life.</dd>\n          <dt>Bone-dry</dt>    <dd>Marks stay crisp like pen. ~0.2 second half-life. Most performant.</dd>\n        </dl>\n        <p>\n          Faster evaporation reclaims active cells sooner, which\n          reclaims simulation cost \u2014 useful as a performance lever for\n          heavy animations and visualizations, at the cost of less\n          wet-on-wet bleed.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"toggles\">Toggles</h2>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Edge darkening</dt>      <dd>Concentrates pigment at the boundaries of wet regions \u2014 the way real watercolor pools at edges when water dries. On by default.</dd>\n          <dt>Pause drying</dt>        <dd>Stops evaporation entirely. Pigment stays suspended indefinitely.</dd>\n          <dt>Auto-dry background</dt> <dd>Settles the canvas instantly when a Background wash completes. Saves substantial CPU when running visualizations on top of a finished wash.</dd>\n          <dt>Fade painting</dt>       <dd>Slowly fades deposited pigment back toward paper. Half-life adjustable via the slider.</dd>\n          <dt>Transparent</dt>         <dd>Makes the body background transparent so the canvas layers over the page background. Useful for embedding.</dd>\n          <dt>WebGL render</dt>        <dd>Routes the per-pixel render through a WebGL2 fragment shader instead of the CPU. Falls back silently if WebGL2 isn't available.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"actions\">Actions</h2>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Re-wet</dt>      <dd>Restores wetness across the whole canvas and lifts 30% of deposited pigment back into suspension \u2014 dried strokes become mobile again.</dd>\n          <dt>Dry</dt>         <dd>Instantly evaporates all water. Suspended pigment locks in place as deposited.</dd>\n          <dt>Remove mask</dt> <dd>Clears all masking fluid in one pass.</dd>\n          <dt>Export PNG</dt>  <dd>Saves the current canvas. Composites paper color underneath unless Transparent is on.</dd>\n          <dt>Reset</dt>       <dd>Clears all pigment, returns the canvas to fresh wet paper.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"performance\">Performance overlay</h2>\n        <p>\n          Hidden by default. Enable two ways:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// URL parameter\n?perf=1\n\n// Console\nWatercolor.perf(true);</pre>\n        <p>\n          The overlay shows FPS, p50/p95/p99 frame times, per-phase ms\n          breakdown (sim, render, anim+viz+svg, wash+fade), active cell\n          count, heap delta (Chromium-only), and the Long Animation\n          Frames counter. Refreshes at 10 Hz; zero overhead when off.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== API ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\">\n        <h1 id=\"api\">Programmatic API</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          Everything in the UI is also reachable through\n          <code>window.Watercolor</code>. Drive the canvas from the\n          browser console, an external script, or an automation file.\n          Designed to be small, composable, and discoverable.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-quickstart\">Quickstart</h2>\n        <p>\n          Open DevTools (F12 or \u2318\u2325I), paste these into the console one\n          at a time, and watch the canvas:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Set a sunset wash\nWatercolor.setBackground('sunset');\n\n// Stamp a rainbow brush at canvas center\nWatercolor.cursor({ color: 'rainbow', size: 40 }).stamp();\n\n// Six cursors painting in a hexagonal orbit\nconst cs = Array.from({length: 6}, () =&gt; Watercolor.cursor({ color: 'blue', size: 18 }));\nlet t = 0;\nsetInterval(() =&gt; {\n  t += 0.03;\n  const { width, height } = Watercolor.grid;\n  cs.forEach((c, i) =&gt; {\n    const angle = t + i * Math.PI / 3;\n    const r = 200 + Math.sin(t * 2) * 80;\n    c.move(width/2 + Math.cos(angle)*r, height/2 + Math.sin(angle)*r).stamp();\n  });\n}, 33);</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-cursors\">Cursors</h2>\n        <p>\n          Cursors are programmable brushes. They hold position and\n          appearance state; they never paint on their own \u2014 call\n          <code>.stamp()</code> when you want a deposit. Many cursors\n          can coexist; the caller's references keep them alive.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Constructor</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.cursor(opts)</span> \u2192 cursor handle</pre>\n        <p>Accepted options (all optional):</p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt><code>color</code></dt>  <dd>One of <code>'rose'</code>, <code>'yellow'</code>, <code>'blue'</code> (alias <code>'cerulean'</code>), <code>'rainbow'</code>, <code>'water'</code>, <code>'lift'</code>, <code>'mask'</code>. Default <code>'blue'</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>size</code></dt>     <dd>Brush diameter in display pixels. Default 16.</dd>\n          <dt><code>strength</code></dt> <dd>Deposit strength per stamp. Default 0.35. Range 0&ndash;1.</dd>\n          <dt><code>x</code>, <code>y</code></dt> <dd>Grid coordinates. Default canvas center. See <a href=\"#api-coords\">coordinates</a>.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h3>Methods</h3>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt><code>cursor.move(x, y)</code></dt> <dd>Update position in grid coordinates. Returns the cursor for chaining.</dd>\n          <dt><code>cursor.set({...})</code></dt> <dd>Update one or more of <code>color</code>, <code>size</code>, <code>strength</code>, <code>x</code>, <code>y</code>. Returns the cursor.</dd>\n          <dt><code>cursor.stamp()</code></dt>   <dd>Paint once at the current position with current properties. Returns the cursor.</dd>\n          <dt><code>cursor.remove()</code></dt>  <dd>Detach from the active set. Optional &mdash; just dropping the reference also works.</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h3>Read-only properties</h3>\n        <p>\n          <code>cursor.id</code>, <code>cursor.x</code>,\n          <code>cursor.y</code>, <code>cursor.size</code>,\n          <code>cursor.strength</code>, <code>cursor.color</code>.\n        </p>\n\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.cursors()</span> \u2192 cursor[]</pre>\n        <p>Returns an array of all currently-alive cursor handles.</p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-modes\">Modes</h2>\n        <p>\n          The three composition layers \u2014 animations, visualizations,\n          backgrounds \u2014 each have a get/set pair. The <code>set</code>\n          variants accept <code>{replace: bool}</code>; default\n          <code>replace: true</code> mirrors the dropdown behavior\n          (switching presets clears the old one's state).\n        </p>\n\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.setAnimation(name, opts?)</span>\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.getAnimation()</span> \u2192 string\n\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.setVisualization(name, opts?)</span>\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.getVisualization()</span> \u2192 string\n\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.setBackground(name)</span>\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.isBackgroundRunning()</span> \u2192 bool</pre>\n\n        <p>Valid names:</p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Animations</dt>      <dd><code>'off'</code>, <code>'ai'</code>, <code>'rainy'</code>, <code>'sunny'</code>, <code>'windy'</code>, <code>'thunderstorm'</code>, <code>'partlyCloudy'</code>, <code>'snowing'</code>, <code>'snowingAdditive'</code>, <code>'tornado'</code></dd>\n          <dt>Visualizations</dt>  <dd><code>'off'</code>, <code>'kaleidoscope'</code>, <code>'lissajous'</code>, <code>'flowField'</code>, <code>'pulse'</code></dd>\n          <dt>Backgrounds</dt>     <dd><code>'day'</code>, <code>'dawn'</code>, <code>'sunset'</code>, <code>'night'</code>, <code>'tornado'</code></dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-text-svg\">Text &amp; SVG</h2>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.paintText(text, opts?)</span> \u2192 number of dabs\n\nopts: { x, y, fontSize, sampleStep, strength, pigment }</pre>\n        <p>\n          Renders text at the given grid coordinates. <code>pigment</code>\n          accepts a color name string (same as cursor); defaults to the\n          UI's currently-selected brush.\n        </p>\n\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">await Watercolor.traceSVG(source)</span> \u2192 points queued\n\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.cancelSVGTrace()</span></pre>\n        <p>\n          <code>source</code> accepts SVG markup (a string starting\n          with <code>&lt;</code>), a URL string (fetched then parsed),\n          or a <code>File</code> object (e.g. from a file picker).\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-toggles\">Toggles</h2>\n        <p>\n          All toggles follow the same pattern: pass a boolean to set,\n          omit to read.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Watercolor.edgeDarkening();        // current state\nWatercolor.edgeDarkening(false);   // disable\n\nWatercolor.pauseDrying(true);\nWatercolor.autoDryBackground(true);\nWatercolor.fadePainting(true);\nWatercolor.fadeFullRender(true);   // re-render whole canvas on fade tick\nWatercolor.transparent(true);\nWatercolor.webgl(true);            // enable WebGL render path\nWatercolor.webglDebugTint(true);   // visible green tint for verification</pre>\n\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.evaporation(value)</span></pre>\n        <p>\n          Accepts a paper-wetness preset name (<code>'flooded'</code>,\n          <code>'wetOnWet'</code>, <code>'damp'</code>,\n          <code>'dryBrush'</code>, <code>'boneDry'</code>) or a numeric\n          multiplier for advanced control. Called with no arg, returns\n          the current effective multiplier.\n        </p>\n\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Watercolor.paperWetnessPresets();\n// \u2192 { flooded: 1, wetOnWet: 3, damp: 8, dryBrush: 18, boneDry: 50 }</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-actions\">Actions</h2>\n        <p>All chainable \u2014 each returns <code>Watercolor</code>.</p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Watercolor.rewet();\nWatercolor.dry();\nWatercolor.removeMask();\nWatercolor.exportPNG();\nWatercolor.reset();\n\n// Chain them\nWatercolor.reset().setBackground('night').dry();</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-perf\">Performance</h2>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.perf(v?)</span></pre>\n        <p>\n          Toggle or read the performance overlay state. The overlay\n          shows p50/p95/p99 frame times, per-phase ms breakdown,\n          active cell count, heap delta (Chromium-only), and Long\n          Animation Frames.\n        </p>\n\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.webglAvailable()</span> \u2192 bool</pre>\n        <p>\n          Tests for WebGL2 support. Lazy-inits on first call. Use this\n          before calling <code>Watercolor.webgl(true)</code> to know\n          whether the toggle will succeed.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-coords\">Coordinates</h2>\n        <p>\n          The canvas has internal <strong>grid coordinates</strong>\n          (0..GW \u00d7 0..GH) which is what cursors and <code>paintAt</code>\n          use. <strong>Display coordinates</strong> (CSS pixels) are\n          what pointer events come in as. Helpers convert between them:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">Watercolor.grid;\n// \u2192 { width: 1152, height: 648 }   (varies with resolution slider)\n\nWatercolor.toGrid(displayX, displayY);\n// \u2192 { x, y } in grid units\n\nWatercolor.toDisplay(gridX, gridY);\n// \u2192 { x, y } in display pixels</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-state\">State snapshot</h2>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.state()</span> \u2192 object</pre>\n        <p>\n          Snapshot of top-level simulation state. Useful for debugging.\n          Does not include the giant Float32Arrays.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">{\n  animation: 'rainy',\n  visualization: 'kaleidoscope',\n  backgroundRunning: false,\n  svgTracing: false,\n  svgTraceRemaining: 0,\n  cursorsActive: 6,\n  maskActive: false,\n  gridWidth: 1152,\n  gridHeight: 648,\n  edgeDarkening: true,\n  dryingPaused: false,\n  autoDryBackground: false,\n  fadePainting: false,\n  transparent: false\n}</pre>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CONCEPTS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\">\n        <h1 id=\"concepts\">Concepts</h1>\n\n        <h2 id=\"concept-physics\">The physics</h2>\n        <p>\n          Each cell on the grid carries seven values:\n          <code>wet</code> (water amount),\n          <code>g</code> (suspended pigment, 3 channels),\n          <code>d</code> (deposited pigment, 3 channels),\n          <code>u</code> and <code>v</code> (velocity components),\n          <code>pressure</code> (drives flow),\n          <code>paperH</code> (paper height noise for granulation),\n          and <code>mask</code> (masking fluid coverage).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Every sim step runs these phases in order:\n        </p>\n        <ol class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><strong>applyEdgeDarkening</strong> \u2014 gradient of wet against neighbors creates pressure that pulls pigment outward (the dark ring at wash edges).</li>\n          <li><strong>updateVelocity</strong> \u2014 pressure gradient drives <code>u</code> and <code>v</code>. Wet cells flow; dry cells are walls.</li>\n          <li><strong>movePigment</strong> \u2014 advection by the velocity field, then isotropic diffusion. Suspended pigment moves; deposited pigment doesn't.</li>\n          <li><strong>transferPigment</strong> \u2014 adsorption (suspended \u2192 deposited at rate proportional to <code>1 - h\u00b7granulation</code>) and desorption (deposited \u2192 suspended at rate <code>staining</code>). The granulation term makes paper-texture variation cause variable deposition.</li>\n          <li><strong>diffuseWet</strong> \u2014 capillary spread of water through paper fibers via Laplacian diffusion.</li>\n          <li><strong>evaporate</strong> \u2014 water decays exponentially. Cells that fall below the dry threshold settle their suspended pigment to deposited.</li>\n        </ol>\n        <p>\n          The render phase then computes per-pixel color via\n          Kubelka-Munk: weighted K and S from the three pigments,\n          run through the sinh/cosh-based reflectance formula,\n          composited over the paper substrate.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"concept-active-rect\">Active rect</h2>\n        <p>\n          A bounding box of cells with active dynamics (wet &gt;\n          threshold). Every sim phase iterates only inside this rect.\n          On a fresh canvas with one dab, the rect might be 200\u00d7200\n          cells \u2014 out of 1152\u00d7648 = 746,000 total. The simulation\n          isn't doing nothing on the unpainted area; it's doing\n          <em>literally zero work</em> there.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The rect grows when paint lands outside it (via\n          <code>expandActiveRect</code> inside <code>paintAt</code>).\n          It shrinks periodically when all cells along an edge have\n          dried out. The render is also rect-localized \u2014 only cells\n          inside the rect get re-drawn each frame.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"concept-mask-rect\">Mask rect</h2>\n        <p>\n          Same idea as the active rect, but tracking the bounding box\n          of masked cells. Without this optimization, every sim phase\n          paid a per-cell mask check across the entire active rect,\n          even when the mask covered &lt;1% of cells. With it, the\n          mask check is restricted to the small region where the mask\n          actually lives.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          A small mask (625 cells in a 462,681-cell active rect) now\n          adds <strong>\u22121.1% overhead</strong> to <code>movePigment</code>\n          \u2014 within measurement noise. Previously the same scenario\n          would have added 80\u2013100% overhead.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"concept-webgl\">WebGL render path</h2>\n        <p>\n          An optional fragment-shader implementation of the render\n          phase, toggleable via the <strong>WebGL render</strong>\n          button. The shader mirrors the CPU code's Kubelka-Munk\n          math bit-for-bit (verified across 792 sweep points; max\n          difference 3.3e-16).\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          Simulation phases still run on the CPU in this version;\n          state arrays are uploaded as textures each render frame.\n          Future v0.20.x releases will migrate individual sim phases\n          to compute over textures, with the perf overlay measuring\n          each migration's impact.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          The toggle silently no-ops if WebGL2 isn't available. The\n          tooltip updates to reflect the unavailable state.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== REFERENCES ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\">\n        <h1 id=\"references\">References</h1>\n        <ol class=\"docs-references\">\n          <li>\n            <strong>Curtis, C. J., Anderson, S. E., Seims, J. E., Fleischer, K. W., &amp; Salesin, D. H.</strong>\n            \"Computer-Generated Watercolor.\" SIGGRAPH '97 Proceedings.\n            <em>The foundation paper. Three-layer water/pigment/paper model with capillary diffusion, edge darkening, and Kubelka-Munk compositing. Figure 5 lists the K and S values used here.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Foster, N., &amp; Metaxas, D.</strong>\n            \"Realistic Animation of Liquids.\" Graphics Interface '96.\n            <em>Staggered-grid fluid scheme; basis for the velocity update.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Stam, J.</strong>\n            \"Stable Fluids.\" SIGGRAPH '99.\n            <em>Modern numerical-stability conventions for the advection step.</em>\n          </li>\n          <li>\n            <strong>Kubelka, P.</strong>\n            \"New contributions to the optics of intensely light-scattering materials.\" JOSA 1954.\n            <em>The reflectance compositing formula used in the render path.</em>\n          </li>\n        </ol>\n\n        <p class=\"docs-footer\">\n          Built iteratively from v0.1 to v0.21. Single HTML file,\n          no dependencies, no build step. View source for the full\n          implementation.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      </main>\n    </div>\n  </section>",

    "input": {
      "description": "Add accordion + scroll-spy + active-link logic",
      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// DOCUMENTATION VIEW (v0.21)\n// ============================================================\n// Two responsibilities below:\n//\n//   1. body.docs-visible class \u2014 set when the user scrolls into the\n//      docs section, hides all canvas chrome (panel, bars, etc.).\n//\n//   2. Accordion sidebar \u2014 section headers toggle their children;\n//      the section currently visible in the viewport auto-expands;\n//      the link matching the visible heading gets an .active class\n//      for visual orientation.\n//\n// IntersectionObserver is feature-checked so the script still parses\n// cleanly in test environments without the browser API.\n\nif (typeof IntersectionObserver !== 'undefined') {\n  // (1) Hide canvas chrome when docs are in view.\n  const docsObserver = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      if (entry.isIntersecting) {\n        document.body.classList.add('docs-visible');\n      } else {\n        document.body.classList.remove('docs-visible');\n      }\n    }\n  }, { threshold: 0.25 });\n  const docsEl = document.getElementById('docs');\n  if (docsEl) docsObserver.observe(docsEl);\n\n  // (2) Accordion behavior on the docs nav. Each section button has\n  // aria-expanded; its parent .docs-nav-group has a [data-expanded]\n  // attribute the CSS keys off for the slide animation. Toggling\n  // either updates both.\n  const navGroups = document.querySelectorAll('.docs-nav-group');\n  function setGroupExpanded(group, expanded) {\n    group.setAttribute('data-expanded', expanded ? 'true' : 'false');\n    const btn = group.querySelector('.docs-nav-section');\n    if (btn) btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', expanded ? 'true' : 'false');\n  }\n  // Initialize: respect the HTML's initial aria-expanded value, but\n  // ensure exactly one group is open (the first one, \"Introduction\")\n  // for a clean starting state.\n  let initialOpenFound = false;\n  navGroups.forEach((group) => {\n    const btn = group.querySelector('.docs-nav-section');\n    const isOpen = btn && btn.getAttribute('aria-expanded') === 'true';\n    if (isOpen && !initialOpenFound) {\n      setGroupExpanded(group, true);\n      initialOpenFound = true;\n    } else {\n      setGroupExpanded(group, false);\n    }\n    // Click handler \u2014 toggle this group, leave others alone.\n    if (btn) {\n      btn.addEventListener('click', () => {\n        const nowOpen = group.getAttribute('data-expanded') !== 'true';\n        setGroupExpanded(group, nowOpen);\n      });\n    }\n  });\n\n  // Scroll spy \u2014 as the user scrolls between docs-page sections,\n  // auto-expand the currently-visible one and collapse the others.\n  // Uses a separate IntersectionObserver scoped to docs-page elements.\n  // rootMargin keeps the \"active\" zone centered in the viewport so\n  // a section becomes active when its heading is roughly screen-\n  // middle, not the moment any pixel enters from the bottom.\n  const docsSpyObserver = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {\n    // Multiple sections can intersect at once during fast scrolls.\n    // Pick the one with the largest intersection ratio as \"active\".\n    let bestEntry = null;\n    let bestRatio = 0;\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      if (entry.isIntersecting && entry.intersectionRatio > bestRatio) {\n        bestEntry = entry;\n        bestRatio = entry.intersectionRatio;\n      }\n    }\n    if (!bestEntry) return;\n    const sectionKey = bestEntry.target.getAttribute('data-section');\n    if (!sectionKey) return;\n    navGroups.forEach((group) => {\n      const isActive = group.getAttribute('data-section') === sectionKey;\n      setGroupExpanded(group, isActive);\n    });\n  }, {\n    rootMargin: '-30% 0px -50% 0px',\n    threshold: [0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1],\n  });\n  document.querySelectorAll('.docs-page[data-section]').forEach((page) => {\n    docsSpyObserver.observe(page);\n  });\n\n  // Active-link highlight. Watch each h1/h2/h3 with an id; the one\n  // closest to the top of the viewport gets its matching nav link\n  // marked .active. Implemented with a second observer for cleaner\n  // separation from the group-level scroll spy above.\n  const headings = document.querySelectorAll(\n    '.docs-main h1[id], .docs-main h2[id], .docs-main h3[id]');\n  const navLinksById = {};\n  document.querySelectorAll('.docs-nav-item').forEach((link) => {\n    const href = link.getAttribute('href');\n    if (href && href.startsWith('#')) {\n      navLinksById[href.slice(1)] = link;\n    }\n  });\n  // Track which heading is most recently above the viewport's top\n  // edge \u2014 that's the \"current section\" the user is reading.\n  let activeLinkId = null;\n  function setActiveLink(id) {\n    if (id === activeLinkId) return;\n    if (activeLinkId && navLinksById[activeLinkId]) {\n      navLinksById[activeLinkId].classList.remove('active');\n    }\n    activeLinkId = id;\n    if (id && navLinksById[id]) {\n      navLinksById[id].classList.add('active');\n    }\n  }\n  const headingObserver = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {\n    // Find the heading nearest the top with positive boundingClientRect.top\n    // OR the topmost visible one. The negative rootMargin top creates\n    // an \"activation line\" 90px below the viewport top \u2014 the heading\n    // crossing this line is treated as the current section.\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      if (entry.isIntersecting) {\n        setActiveLink(entry.target.id);\n        return;\n      }\n    }\n  }, {\n    rootMargin: '-90px 0px -70% 0px',\n    threshold: 0,\n  });\n  headings.forEach((h) => headingObserver.observe(h));\n}",

      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// DOCUMENTATION VIEW (v0.21)\n// ============================================================\n// Two responsibilities below:\n//\n//   1. body.docs-visible class \u2014 set when the user scrolls into the\n//      docs section, hides all canvas chrome (panel, bars, etc.).\n//\n//   2. Accordion sidebar \u2014 section headers toggle their children;\n//      the section currently visible in the viewport auto-expands;\n//      the link matching the visible heading gets an .active class\n//      for visual orientation.\n//\n// IntersectionObserver is feature-checked so the script still parses\n// cleanly in test environments without the browser API.\n\nif (typeof IntersectionObserver !== 'undefined') {\n  // (1) Hide canvas chrome when docs are in view.\n  const docsObserver = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      if (entry.isIntersecting) {\n        document.body.classList.add('docs-visible');\n      } else {\n        document.body.classList.remove('docs-visible');\n      }\n    }\n  }, { threshold: 0.25 });\n  const docsEl = document.getElementById('docs');\n  if (docsEl) docsObserver.observe(docsEl);\n\n  // (2) Accordion behavior on the docs nav. Each section button has\n  // aria-expanded; its parent .docs-nav-group has a [data-expanded]\n  // attribute the CSS keys off for the slide animation. Toggling\n  // either updates both.\n  const navGroups = document.querySelectorAll('.docs-nav-group');\n  function setGroupExpanded(group, expanded) {\n    group.setAttribute('data-expanded', expanded ? 'true' : 'false');\n    const btn = group.querySelector('.docs-nav-section');\n    if (btn) btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', expanded ? 'true' : 'false');\n  }\n  // Initialize: respect the HTML's initial aria-expanded value, but\n  // ensure exactly one group is open (the first one, \"Introduction\")\n  // for a clean starting state.\n  let initialOpenFound = false;\n  navGroups.forEach((group) => {\n    const btn = group.querySelector('.docs-nav-section');\n    const isOpen = btn && btn.getAttribute('aria-expanded') === 'true';\n    if (isOpen && !initialOpenFound) {\n      setGroupExpanded(group, true);\n      initialOpenFound = true;\n    } else {\n      setGroupExpanded(group, false);\n    }\n    // Click handler \u2014 toggle this group, leave others alone.\n    if (btn) {\n      btn.addEventListener('click', () => {\n        const nowOpen = group.getAttribute('data-expanded') !== 'true';\n        setGroupExpanded(group, nowOpen);\n      });\n    }\n  });\n\n  // Scroll spy \u2014 as the user scrolls between docs-page sections,\n  // auto-expand the currently-visible one and collapse the others.\n  // rootMargin keeps the \"active\" zone centered so a section becomes\n  // active when its content is roughly screen-middle, not the moment\n  // any pixel enters from the bottom.\n  const docsSpyObserver = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {\n    // Multiple sections can intersect at once during fast scrolls.\n    // Pick the one with the largest intersection ratio as \"active\".\n    let bestEntry = null;\n    let bestRatio = 0;\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      if (entry.isIntersecting && entry.intersectionRatio > bestRatio) {\n        bestEntry = entry;\n        bestRatio = entry.intersectionRatio;\n      }\n    }\n    if (!bestEntry) return;\n    const sectionKey = bestEntry.target.getAttribute('data-section');\n    if (!sectionKey) return;\n    navGroups.forEach((group) => {\n      const isActive = group.getAttribute('data-section') === sectionKey;\n      setGroupExpanded(group, isActive);\n    });\n  }, {\n    rootMargin: '-30% 0px -50% 0px',\n    threshold: [0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1],\n  });\n  document.querySelectorAll('.docs-page[data-section]').forEach((page) => {\n    docsSpyObserver.observe(page);\n  });\n\n  // Active-link highlight. Watch each h1/h2/h3 with an id; whichever\n  // is crossing an \"activation line\" 90px below the viewport top is\n  // the section being read, and its matching nav link gets .active.\n  const headings = document.querySelectorAll(\n    '.docs-main h1[id], .docs-main h2[id], .docs-main h3[id]');\n  const navLinksById = {};\n  document.querySelectorAll('.docs-nav-item').forEach((link) => {\n    const href = link.getAttribute('href');\n    if (href && href.startsWith('#')) {\n      navLinksById[href.slice(1)] = link;\n    }\n  });\n  let activeLinkId = null;\n  function setActiveLink(id) {\n    if (id === activeLinkId) return;\n    if (activeLinkId && navLinksById[activeLinkId]) {\n      navLinksById[activeLinkId].classList.remove('active');\n    }\n    activeLinkId = id;\n    if (id && navLinksById[id]) {\n      navLinksById[id].classList.add('active');\n    }\n  }\n  const headingObserver = new IntersectionObserver((entries) => {\n    for (const entry of entries) {\n      if (entry.isIntersecting) {\n        setActiveLink(entry.target.id);\n        return;\n      }\n    }\n  }, {\n    rootMargin: '-90px 0px -70% 0px',\n    threshold: 0,\n  });\n  headings.forEach((h) => headingObserver.observe(h));\n}",

    "text": "**v0.21 updated \u2014 accordion docs nav**\n\n**What you'll see:**\n\nEach section in the sidebar is now an accordion button with a chevron (\u203a) on the right:\n- **Click section header** to expand/collapse its child links\n- **Chevron rotates 90\u00b0** when expanded\n- **Smooth slide animation** (max-height + opacity transition)\n- **Hairline divider** between groups for visual rhythm\n\n**Auto-expand on scroll:**\n\nAs you scroll through the docs, the section containing the currently-visible content automatically expands and the others collapse. Implemented via a second `IntersectionObserver` scoped to `.docs-page[data-section]` elements, with `rootMargin: '-30% 0px -50% 0px'` so a section becomes \"active\" when its content is roughly screen-middle, not the moment any pixel enters the viewport.\n\nWhen multiple sections intersect at once (fast scrolls), the one with the largest intersection ratio wins.\n\n**Active link highlighting:**\n\nA third observer watches every `h1`, `h2`, `h3` with an id. Whichever heading is crossing an \"activation line\" 90px below the viewport top gets its matching nav link marked `.active` (slight font-weight bump + full opacity). Gives you orientation within the section \u2014 you can see exactly which subsection you're reading.\n\n**Sticky behavior:**\n\nSidebar uses `position: sticky; top: 32px` (same as before) so it follows the viewport. New: `max-height: calc(100vh - 64px); overflow-y: auto` caps it to the viewport height. If your expanded section's child list is long, the sidebar scrolls internally rather than getting cut off or pushing layout. The scrollbar is invisible by default and fades in on hover \u2014 present but quiet.\n\n**Accessibility:**\n\n- Native `<button>` elements for accordion headers (keyboard accessible by default)\n- `aria-expanded` toggles in sync with the `data-expanded` CSS hook\n- Chevron has `aria-hidden=\"true\"` so screen readers ignore the decorative arrow\n\nTry it: scroll down, navigate around. Click any section header to fold/unfold. Click a child link to jump and watch the active highlight follow.",

The MDN docs say this exactly: *\"Setting overflow on one axis (overflow-x or overflow-y) automatically sets the other axis to auto, which is enough to break position: sticky on descendants.\"*\n\nWhen `overflow-x: hidden` is set, the browser implicitly sets `overflow-y: auto` on that element, which makes *it* the scroll container. Then `position: sticky` inside it sticks relative to that container's scroll, not the viewport.\n\nThe fix is to remove `overflow-x: hidden` from html/body. To prevent horizontal scroll without breaking sticky, I can clip it on `.app-section` (which is already `overflow: hidden`) and on `.docs` separately:",

    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T16:51:22.655827Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.21 updated \u2014 sticky sidebar fixed**\n\n**The bug:** `html, body { overflow-x: hidden }` was breaking `position: sticky` on the docs sidebar.\n\nThis is a well-known CSS gotcha: when you set `overflow-x` (or `overflow-y`) on an element, the browser implicitly sets the other axis to `auto`, which makes *that element* the scroll container. Once that happens, `position: sticky` on any descendant sticks relative to that scroll container's internal scroll position \u2014 not the viewport. Since the body wasn't actually scrolling (the *page* was), the sidebar had no scroll to stick against, so it just rode the page like any normal element.\n\n**The fix:** removed `overflow-x: hidden` from `html, body` entirely. The page-level scrolling is now attached to the viewport, where it belongs, so `position: sticky` works correctly.\n\nTo prevent horizontal scroll without re-breaking sticky, I used **`overflow-x: clip`** on `.docs` instead of `hidden`. The difference:\n\n| Rule | Clips overflow? | Creates scroll container? |\n|---|---|---|\n| `overflow-x: hidden` | Yes | **Yes** (breaks sticky) |\n| `overflow-x: clip` | Yes | **No** (sticky still works) |\n\n`clip` is the modern CSS spec'd specifically for this use case \u2014 visual clipping without the side effect of becoming a scroll container. Supported in Safari 16+, Chrome 90+, Firefox 81+ (all of which support WebGL2, so anyone running this app has it).\n\n`.app-section` still has `overflow: hidden` because it's a separate scroll context above the docs and doesn't contain the sticky sidebar.\n\nThe sidebar should now stay properly glued to the top of the viewport (with the `top: 32px` offset) as you scroll through the documentation. \ud83c\udfaf",
    "citations": []
  }
]

Scales all three water-brush\n    // deposits together (wet, pressure, lift) so the user can dial\n    // \"wetter\" or \"drier\" water with a single control. The lift\n    // amount has to be clamped to 1.0 because it's a fraction (you\n    // can't lift more than 100% of what's there); the wet+pressure\n    // values clamp via the existing per-cell maxima.\n    const wetGain  = 0.55 * waterLoadMult;\n    const presGain = 0.18 * waterLoadMult;\n    const liftGain = Math.min(1.0, 0.18 * waterLoadMult);\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - gx, dy = py - gy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        // Masked cells resist water \u2014 masking fluid is hydrophobic.\n        if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        const falloff = 1 - Math.sqrt(d2) / r;\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        const ww = wet[i] + wetGain * falloff;\n        wet[i] = ww > 1 ? 1 : ww;\n        pressure[i] += presGain * f2;\n        // Localized lift of deposited pigment. Per-frame fraction is\n        // gentler than the global re-wet (which is one-shot at 0.30);\n        // here the user can hold/drag to escalate.\n        const liftFrac = liftGain * f2;\n        if (liftFrac > 0) {\n          for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n            const dval = d[k][i];\n            if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n            const lift = dval * liftFrac;\n            d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n            const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n            g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : nv;\n          }\n        }\n      }\n    }\n    return;\n  }",

Both\n          default to <code>1.0</code> and accept floats; the UI sliders\n          clamp them to sensible ranges (paint 0.1&ndash;3, water 0.2&ndash;3),\n          but the API doesn't enforce a hard cap \u2014 extreme values just\n          saturate the per-cell maxima.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"><span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.paintLoad(mult?)</span>\n<span class=\"docs-sig\">Watercolor.waterLoad(mult?)</span>\n\n// Examples\nWatercolor.paintLoad(2);     // dense, saturated pigment\nWatercolor.paintLoad(0.3);   // pale, sparse pigment\nWatercolor.waterLoad(2.5);   // very wet water brush \u2014 big pools\nWatercolor.waterLoad(0.5);   // damp water brush \u2014 gentle lifting</pre>\n        <p>\n          <code>paintLoad</code> affects pigment brushes (rose, yellow,\n          blue) and rainbow. <code>waterLoad</code> only affects the\n          water brush \u2014 it scales wet, pressure, and lift together,\n          so a higher value means bigger pools, more bleeding, and\n          more pigment re-mobilized from dry strokes.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"api-actions\">Actions</h2>",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.22 \u2014 Paint load + Water load sliders**\n\nTwo new sliders in the brush-controls row, sitting between **Brush** and **Resolution**:\n\n| Slider | Range | Default | Affects |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| **Paint load** | 0.1\u00d7 \u2013 3\u00d7 | 1.0\u00d7 | Pigment brushes (rose, yellow, blue) and rainbow |\n| **Water load** | 0.2\u00d7 \u2013 3\u00d7 | 1.0\u00d7 | Water brush only |\n\n**Paint load** is the simpler control: it multiplies the per-dab pigment strength. At 0.1\u00d7 you get pale, sparse paint that needs many overlaps to build up; at 3\u00d7 you get dense, saturated dabs that fill in fast. The behavioral test confirms exactly 2\u00d7 output at 2\u00d7 input \u2014 direct linear scaling.\n\n**Water load** is more interesting: it scales all three water-brush effects together (wet, pressure, lift) so increasing it is genuinely \"more wet\" in a watercolor-physical sense. Larger pools spread further (wet), the surrounding bleed is more dramatic (pressure), and *more dry pigment gets re-mobilized into the wet layer* (lift). At 2\u00d7 the actual wet delta scales 1.48\u00d7 because individual cells saturate at `wet = 1` \u2014 you can't make a cell more than 100% wet, so the gain caps out gracefully. This is correct: a wetter brush makes a *bigger wet area*, not a more-than-wet cell.\n\n**API:**\n\n```js\nWatercolor.paintLoad();       // returns current (default 1.0)\nWatercolor.paintLoad(2);      // dense pigment\nWatercolor.paintLoad(0.3);    // sparse, watery\n\nWatercolor.waterLoad(2.5);    // very wet \u2014 big pools, dramatic bleed\nWatercolor.waterLoad(0.5);    // damp \u2014 gentle lifting only\n\n// Chains\nWatercolor.paintLoad(1.5).waterLoad(2);\n```\n\nBoth are documented in the **Programmatic API \u2192 Toggles** section of the docs (under a new \"Brush load\" subsection with examples).\n\n**Try this:**\n- Pick **rose**, drag **Paint load** to 3.0, paint a stroke \u2192 instantly dense\n- Pick **water**, drag **Water load** to 3.0, drag across an existing painted area \u2192 much wetter pool that pulls more pigment into a bleed\n- Combine with **Paper wetness: Flooded** for maximally watercolor behavior\n\nBoth sliders preserve the original aesthetic at 1\u00d7 \u2014 no existing painting feel breaks.",

  }

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T16:58:05.976989Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T16:58:19.661636Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "text",
    "text": " Good question \u2014 let me trace through the code to answer accurately rather than guess.\n\nLooking at `paintAt`:\n- The **MASK_INDEX** branch ignores both\n- The **WATER_INDEX** branch uses `waterLoadMult` only (and ignores the `strength` argument entirely \u2014 it has its own hardcoded values)\n- The **LIFT_INDEX** branch uses neither (subtraction-only, no strength parameter)\n- The **RAINBOW_INDEX** branch uses the `strength` argument\n- The **regular pigment** branch (fall-through for indices 0/1/2) uses the `strength` argument\n\nNow, who calls `paintAt` with what strength?\n\nFor **pigment brushes (0/1/2)**, the call sites are `paintAt(..., currentPigment, 0.38 * paintLoadMult)` (tap) and `paintAt(..., currentPigment, 0.34 * paintLoadMult)` (drag). So pigment brushes get `paintLoad` baked into their strength. **Water load doesn't enter the picture** because they take the pigment branch.\n\nFor **rainbow** specifically: the rainbow brush is selected via `currentPigment = RAINBOW_INDEX`, and the click/drag handlers still call `paintAt(c.gx, c.gy, gridRadius, currentPigment, 0.38 * paintLoadMult)`. The strength passed in is scaled by `paintLoadMult`. Inside paintAt's RAINBOW_INDEX branch, that strength is used directly to deposit pigment. So **rainbow gets paintLoad scaling**.\n\nBut \u2014 there's a subtlety. The rainbow branch *also* adds `wet[i] + 0.45 * falloff` and `pressure[i] += 0.18 * f2`. Those are hardcoded and don't scale with `paintLoad`. Same for the regular pigment branch.\n\nSo the honest answer to your question:\n\n**Pigment brushes (rose/yellow/blue) and rainbow:**\n- **Paint load**: \u2705 Yes \u2014 scales how much pigment lands per dab\n- **Water load**: \u274c No \u2014 has no effect (water load only affects the dedicated water brush)\n\n**Water brush:**\n- **Paint load**: \u274c No \u2014 has no effect (water brush deposits no pigment)\n- **Water load**: \u2705 Yes \u2014 scales wet, pressure, and lift together\n\nSo they don't *combine* \u2014 each slider has its own brush domain.\n\n**Is that the right design?**\n\nIt's defensible \u2014 clean separation, each control affects exactly what its label says. But you're hinting that the user might expect both sliders to interact with a pigment brush, which has an interesting physical interpretation:\n\n- **Paint load** = how much pigment is on the brush\n- **Water load** = how much water is on the brush\n\nA pigment brush physically *does* carry both pigment and water \u2014 that's why pigment brushes implicitly add wet to the cells (`wet[i] + 0.45 * falloff` in the pigment branch). So letting **water load** also scale the wet/pressure contribution of pigment brushes would make the controls more orthogonal and physically grounded:\n\n- `paintLoad=2, waterLoad=1` \u2192 a saturated brush with normal water \u2192 dense pigment, normal bleeding\n- `paintLoad=2, waterLoad=2` \u2192 a saturated *wet* brush \u2192 dense pigment, dramatic bleeding\n- `paintLoad=0.3, waterLoad=2` \u2192 a sparse wet brush \u2192 pale paint, lots of bleed (dry-brush-into-wash effect)\n- `paintLoad=2, waterLoad=0.3` \u2192 a thick dry brush \u2192 saturated paint that barely spreads\n\nWant me to extend `waterLoad` to also scale the pigment-brush's wet/pressure contribution? It would make the two sliders combine for a real \"loading the brush\" model rather than being strictly separate. The change is small \u2014 multiply the `0.45` wet and `0.18` pressure in the pigment + rainbow branches by `waterLoadMult`. Default 1\u00d7 stays identical to current behavior.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

Two lines in each branch went from:\n\n```js\nconst ww = wet[i] + 0.45 * falloff;\npressure[i] += 0.18 * f2;\n```\n\nto:\n\n```js\nconst ww = wet[i] + 0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff;\npressure[i] += 0.18 * waterLoadMult * f2;\n```\n\n**Behaviorally verified** \u2014 the test confirms exact 2\u00d7 pressure scaling at waterLoad=2 for both pigment and rainbow brushes, and that paintLoad and waterLoad combine independently (paintLoad=2, waterLoad=2 \u2192 pigment 2\u00d7, pressure 2\u00d7).\n\n**The physical model now combines cleanly:**\n\n| paintLoad | waterLoad | What you get |\n|---|---|---|\n| 1\u00d7 | 1\u00d7 | Default brush \u2014 same as v0.20 and earlier |\n| 2\u00d7 | 1\u00d7 | Saturated brush, normal water \u2014 dense pigment with normal bleeding |\n| 2\u00d7 | 2\u00d7 | Saturated *wet* brush \u2014 dense pigment with dramatic bleeding |\n| 0.3\u00d7 | 2\u00d7 | Sparse wet brush \u2014 pale paint with lots of bleed (dry-brush-into-wash effect) |\n| 2\u00d7 | 0.3\u00d7 | Thick dry brush \u2014 saturated paint that barely spreads |\n\n**The tooltip and docs both updated** to explain the new combined behavior. Docs section now includes the 4-row table above so users understand each combination.\n\n**Two things to note:**\n\nThe wet field is capped per-cell at 1.0, so at very high waterLoad the wet doesn't keep doubling \u2014 cells just saturate. This is correct (you can't make a cell more than fully wet), it just means the *visible* effect of cranking waterLoad past ~2\u00d7 is mostly through **pressure**, which has no cap and directly drives bleeding via the velocity field.\n\nDefault 1\u00d7 \u00d7 1\u00d7 behaves exactly as before. No existing painting feel breaks.\n\n**Try this for a \"loading the brush\" workflow:**\n\n1. **Wet brush**: paintLoad 1\u00d7, waterLoad 2.5\u00d7, pick rose \u2192 drag a stroke \u2014 watch the dramatic bleed.\n2. **Loaded with paint**: paintLoad 2.5\u00d7, waterLoad 0.5\u00d7, drag \u2192 tight saturated stroke that barely spreads (Eastern brush-pen feel).\n3. **Wet wash brush**: paintLoad 0.5\u00d7, waterLoad 3\u00d7, drag \u2014 barely-pigmented water that spreads dramatically; great for atmospheric backgrounds.",

Let me think through what changed in the last two versions vs. v0.21:\n\n**Things added since v0.21:**\n1. **v0.22**: `paintLoadMult` and `waterLoadMult` state variables (no perf cost \u2014 just globals)\n2. **v0.22**: Sliders + UI wiring (one-time event listener, no per-frame cost)\n3. **v0.22**: Multiplied tap-down + drag-stroke `strength` by `paintLoadMult` (one extra mult per `paintAt` call \u2014 negligible)\n4. **v0.22**: Water brush branch hoisted `wetGain`, `presGain`, `liftGain` outside the loop and scaled them by `waterLoadMult` (actually this is *faster* \u2014 fewer multiplies per cell)\n5. **v0.22.1**: Pigment + rainbow branches scale wet and pressure by `waterLoadMult` (one extra mult per cell inside the loop \u2014 but only during `paintAt`, not during simStep)\n\nNone of those changes should cause a meaningful perf regression. `paintAt` is called once per pointer event (queued) or per animation frame, not in any sim hot loop.\n\n**But wait** \u2014 let me reconsider what might be misleading here. There's a subtler effect:\n\nWhen `paintLoadMult > 1` or `waterLoadMult > 1`, more pigment/water gets deposited per dab. **More wet cells = larger active rect = more cells the sim has to process per frame.** Higher load doesn't make `paintAt` itself slower \u2014 it makes the canvas *busier*, which makes every subsequent `simStep` slower.\n\nSo if you've been painting with paintLoad and waterLoad higher than 1\u00d7 (the new defaults are 1\u00d7, but you might have cranked them), the canvas has accumulated more wet area, the active rect is bigger, and `simStep` does more work per frame. **The perf hit is real but it's downstream of physically more paint, not from the code changes.**\n\nLet me verify by actually running a comparison. A few honest possibilities, ordered roughly by how likely they are:\\\\n\\\\n**1. JavaScriptCore vs V8 on this specific workload.** Safari uses JavaScriptCore; Chrome uses V8. The hot path here is *tight numeric loops over Float32Arrays* \\\\u2014 `diffuseWet`, `movePigment`, `transferPigment`, `render`. V8's TurboFan tends to be more aggressive at unboxing floats and inlining typed-array reads in those exact patterns. JSC is competitive on most code but historically a bit behind on dense numeric loops with branching. This isn't universal \\\\u2014 JSC wins on plenty of workloads \\\\u2014 but for this code it could plausibly favor V8.\\\\n\\\\n**2. `putImageData` / `drawImage` pipeline differences.** The render does a `putImageData` to an offscreen canvas, then `drawImage` it scaled up to the display canvas. Safari and Chrome have different GPU upload paths for this. On retina displays especially, Safari sometimes does extra color-space conversions (P3 vs sRGB) that Chrome skips by default.\\\\n\\\\n**3. Throttling.** Safari throttles `requestAnimationFrame` more aggressively when the tab isn't focused, when battery is low, or under thermal pressure. If \\\\\\\"Claude's browser\\\\\\\" is a fresh Chromium window in the foreground and Safari was backgrounded or had many tabs open, that alone could explain the gap.\\\\n\\\\n**4. Hardware acceleration / Low Power Mode.** Safari on macOS respects Low Power Mode harder than Chrome does. If your laptop is on battery and Low Power is on, Safari will down-clock animation work.\\\\n\\\\n**5. It might not actually be faster \\\\u2014 just smoother.** Different `rAF` cadences feel different even at the same effective FPS. Chrome's frame pacing is generally tighter.\\\\n\\\\nIf you want to actually know, the cheapest check is open both side-by-side, hit the same preset (try **Background: Night + Auto-dry off + Visualization: Kaleidoscope** \\\\u2014 that's the heaviest combo), and watch the dryness/wetness bars. They update at 10 Hz from real frame counts and they'll diverge if one browser is doing fewer sim steps per second. I could also add an FPS readout to the panel if you want to measure rather than guess \\\\u2014 say the word.\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\\n\\n================================================================================\\n\\nHuman:\\nContent:\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"start_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:43.766534Z\\\",\\n    \\\"stop_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:43.766534Z\\\",\\n    \\\"flags\\\": null,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"text\\\",\\n    \\\"text\\\": \\\"Are there other ways to track performance in addition to FPS?\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\\n\\n================================================================================\\n\\nAssistant:\\nContent:\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"start_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:45.658627Z\\\",\\n    \\\"stop_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:59.878565Z\\\",\\n    \\\"flags\\\": null,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"text\\\",\\n    \\\"text\\\": \\\" Yes \\\\u2014 FPS alone is a blunt instrument. Several more revealing metrics for a workload like this:\\\\n\\\\n**Frame time (ms/frame) with percentiles.** Average FPS hides jank. A canvas hitting 60 fps average but with 2% of frames at 80ms feels stuttery; one at 50 fps with tight variance feels smooth. Track p50, p95, p99 frame times in a ring buffer. The gap between p50 and p99 *is* the jank signal.\\\\n\\\\n**Per-phase breakdown.** The frame budget here is spent in clearly separable steps: `applyPendingPaint`, `animationStep`, `visualizationStep`, `svgTraceStep`, `simStep` (\\\\u00d72 when active), `fadeStep`, `timeWashStep`, `render`. Time each with `performance.now()` deltas and you can see which phase is eating budget. Usually it's `render` (full pixel sweep) or `movePigment` (the advection + diffusion loops). Without this you're guessing.\\\\n\\\\n**Active cell count.** This codebase tracks an active rectangle but the actual \\\\\\\"live\\\\\\\" cell count varies wildly \\\\u2014 a fresh canvas with one dab might have 200 live cells; a full Night-wash + Tornado might have 700,000. Cost scales linearly with this. Plotting it alongside frame time tells you whether slowdowns are algorithmic or just the canvas being wetter.\\\\n\\\\n**Allocations per frame.** Garbage collection pauses are a major source of jank. Chrome's `performance.memory.usedJSHeapSize` (non-standard, Chromium-only) sampled per frame shows the sawtooth pattern of allocations + GC. If the sawtooth is sharp, you have an allocation hot spot. The kaleidoscope visualization, for instance, allocates a `getBoundingClientRect()` object every frame \\\\u2014 small but visible.\\\\n\\\\n**Long Animation Frames (LoAF).** Newer Performance Observer API exposes frames that blocked the main thread longer than 50ms with attribution to scripts. `new PerformanceObserver(list => ...)` with `entryTypes: ['long-animation-frame']`. Much better than FPS for catching real user-perceptible jank.\\\\n\\\\n**GPU vs CPU time.** Chrome DevTools' Performance tab shows compositor + raster work separately from main-thread JS. For this canvas, JS dominates, but on a transparent body + drawImage upscale, the compositor sometimes shows up as a meaningful chunk.\\\\n\\\\n**Input latency.** Time from `pointermove` event to next paint that reflects it. The pendingPaint queue in this code accumulates per-event coordinates and flushes once per frame, so latency is bounded at ~1 frame + sim cost. Measure it directly by timestamping the event and subtracting at the next `render` call.\\\\n\\\\n**Visual stability (CLS analog).** Less relevant here than for typical pages, but worth knowing: layout shifts in chrome elements could be measured if you cared.\\\\n\\\\nFor this specific tool the highest-value additions would be **(1) p99 frame time**, **(2) per-phase ms breakdown**, and **(3) active cell count**. Together they tell you not just \\\\\\\"is it slow\\\\\\\" but \\\\\\\"what is slow and why.\\\\\\\" A small overlay readout would be cheap \\\\u2014 maybe 15 lines of code, gated behind a `?perf=1` URL flag so it doesn't bother regular users.\\\\n\\\\nWant me to add that as an option in v0.19?\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\",\"stderr\":\"\"}",

Everything in the UI is accessible programmatically:\\\\n\\\\n**Cursors** \\\\u2014 caller-controlled, never auto-paint:\\\\n```js\\\\nconst c = Watercolor.cursor({ color: 'rose', size: 25, x: 100, y: 200, strength: 0.5 });\\\\nc.move(300, 400);                          // grid coordinates\\\\nc.set({ color: 'rainbow', size: 40 });\\\\nc.stamp();                                  // explicit deposit\\\\nc.remove();                                 // detach\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nColor names: `rose`, `yellow`, `blue` (alias `cerulean`), `rainbow`, `water`, `lift`, `mask`. Caller drives motion \\\\u2014 orbit, follow mouse, choreograph patterns, whatever, by calling `.move()` + `.stamp()` from your own loop.\\\\n\\\\n**Animations / Visualizations** \\\\u2014 per-call replace semantics:\\\\n```js\\\\nWatercolor.setAnimation('tornado');                   // replace = default\\\\nWatercolor.setAnimation('thunderstorm', {replace: false});  // additive\\\\nWatercolor.setVisualization('kaleidoscope');\\\\nWatercolor.setBackground('night');\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\n**SVG** \\\\u2014 accepts markup, URL, or File:\\\\n```js\\\\nawait Watercolor.traceSVG('<svg>\\\\u2026</svg>');\\\\nawait Watercolor.traceSVG('/icons/star.svg');         // fetches\\\\nawait Watercolor.traceSVG(fileFromInputElement);\\\\nWatercolor.cancelSVGTrace();\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\n**Text:**\\\\n```js\\\\nWatercolor.paintText('hello', { fontSize: 120, pigment: 0 });\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\n**Toggles** \\\\u2014 pass bool to set, omit to read:\\\\n```js\\\\nWatercolor.edgeDarkening(false);          // disable\\\\nWatercolor.fadePainting();                // returns current value\\\\nWatercolor.pauseDrying(true);\\\\nWatercolor.autoDryBackground(true);\\\\nWatercolor.transparent(true);\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\n**Imperative actions** \\\\u2014 all chainable:\\\\n```js\\\\nWatercolor.rewet().dry().removeMask().exportPNG().reset();\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\n**Diagnostics:**\\\\n```js\\\\nWatercolor.state();\\\\n// { animation: 'tornado', visualization: 'kaleidoscope', backgroundRunning: false, ... }\\\\nWatercolor.grid;                           // { width: 1152, height: 648 }\\\\nWatercolor.toGrid(mouseX, mouseY);         // event \\\\u2192 grid coords\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nUI stays in sync \\\\u2014 calling `Watercolor.setAnimation('tornado')` updates the dropdown so users can see what's happening from API calls.\\\\n\\\\n**Try this in the console** for a six-cursor radial paint:\\\\n```js\\\\nWatercolor.reset();\\\\nconst cs = Array.from({length:6}, (_,i) => Watercolor.cursor({color: ['rose','yellow','blue'][i%3], size: 18}));\\\\nlet t = 0;\\\\nsetInterval(() => {\\\\n  t += 0.03;\\\\n  const cx = Watercolor.grid.width/2, cy = Watercolor.grid.height/2;\\\\n  cs.forEach((c, i) => {\\\\n    const angle = t + i * Math.PI/3;\\\\n    const r = 200 + Math.sin(t*2) * 80;\\\\n    c.move(cx + Math.cos(angle)*r, cy + Math.sin(angle)*r).stamp();\\\\n  });\\\\n}, 33);\\\\n```\\\\n\\\\nThat's a 6-cursor kaleidoscope coded from scratch in 8 lines, using just the API.\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\\n\\n================================================================================\\n\\nHuman:\\nContent:\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"start_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:50:00.586276Z\\\",\\n    \\\"stop_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:50:00.586276Z\\\",\\n    \\\"flags\\\": null,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"text\\\",\\n    \\\"text\\\": \\\"Why is Claude's browser more performant than Safari's?\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\\n\\n================================================================================\\n\\nAssistant:\\nContent:\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"start_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:50:04.088454Z\\\",\\n    \\\"stop_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:50:15.409139Z\\\",\\n    \\\"flags\\\": null,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"text\\\",\\n    \\\"text\\\": \\\" That's a tough one to answer without measurement \\\\u2014 I don't actually know what you're seeing. A few honest possibilities, ordered roughly by how likely they are:\\\\n\\\\n**1. JavaScriptCore vs V8 on this specific workload.** Safari uses JavaScriptCore; Chrome uses V8. The hot path here is *tight numeric loops over Float32Arrays* \\\\u2014 `diffuseWet`, `movePigment`, `transferPigment`, `render`. V8's TurboFan tends to be more aggressive at unboxing floats and inlining typed-array reads in those exact patterns. JSC is competitive on most code but historically a bit behind on dense numeric loops with branching. This isn't universal \\\\u2014 JSC wins on plenty of workloads \\\\u2014 but for this code it could plausibly favor V8.\\\\n\\\\n**2. `putImageData` / `drawImage` pipeline differences.** The render does a `putImageData` to an offscreen canvas, then `drawImage` it scaled up to the display canvas. Safari and Chrome have different GPU upload paths for this. On retina displays especially, Safari sometimes does extra color-space conversions (P3 vs sRGB) that Chrome skips by default.\\\\n\\\\n**3. Throttling.** Safari throttles `requestAnimationFrame` more aggressively when the tab isn't focused, when battery is low, or under thermal pressure. If \\\\\\\"Claude's browser\\\\\\\" is a fresh Chromium window in the foreground and Safari was backgrounded or had many tabs open, that alone could explain the gap.\\\\n\\\\n**4. Hardware acceleration / Low Power Mode.** Safari on macOS respects Low Power Mode harder than Chrome does. If your laptop is on battery and Low Power is on, Safari will down-clock animation work.\\\\n\\\\n**5. It might not actually be faster \\\\u2014 just smoother.** Different `rAF` cadences feel different even at the same effective FPS. Chrome's frame pacing is generally tighter.\\\\n\\\\nIf you want to actually know, the cheapest check is open both side-by-side, hit the same preset (try **Background: Night + Auto-dry off + Visualization: Kaleidoscope** \\\\u2014 that's the heaviest combo), and watch the dryness/wetness bars. They update at 10 Hz from real frame counts and they'll diverge if one browser is doing fewer sim steps per second. I could also add an FPS readout to the panel if you want to measure rather than guess \\\\u2014 say the word.\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\\n\\n================================================================================\\n\\nHuman:\\nContent:\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"start_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:43.766534Z\\\",\\n    \\\"stop_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:43.766534Z\\\",\\n    \\\"flags\\\": null,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"text\\\",\\n    \\\"text\\\": \\\"Are there other ways to track performance in addition to FPS?\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\\n\\n================================================================================\\n\\nAssistant:\\nContent:\\n[\\n  {\\n    \\\"start_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:45.658627Z\\\",\\n    \\\"stop_timestamp\\\": \\\"2026-05-11T14:59:59.878565Z\\\",\\n    \\\"flags\\\": null,\\n    \\\"type\\\": \\\"text\\\",\\n    \\\"text\\\": \\\" Yes \\\\u2014 FPS alone is a blunt instrument. Several more revealing metrics for a workload like this:\\\\n\\\\n**Frame time (ms/frame) with percentiles.** Average FPS hides jank. A canvas hitting 60 fps average but with 2% of frames at 80ms feels stuttery; one at 50 fps with tight variance feels smooth. Track p50, p95, p99 frame times in a ring buffer. The gap between p50 and p99 *is* the jank signal.\\\\n\\\\n**Per-phase breakdown.** The frame budget here is spent in clearly separable steps: `applyPendingPaint`, `animationStep`, `visualizationStep`, `svgTraceStep`, `simStep` (\\\\u00d72 when active), `fadeStep`, `timeWashStep`, `render`. Time each with `performance.now()` deltas and you can see which phase is eating budget. Usually it's `render` (full pixel sweep) or `movePigment` (the advection + diffusion loops). Without this you're guessing.\\\\n\\\\n**Active cell count.** This codebase tracks an active rectangle but the actual \\\\\\\"live\\\\\\\" cell count varies wildly \\\\u2014 a fresh canvas with one dab might have 200 live cells; a full Night-wash + Tornado might have 700,000. Cost scales linearly with this. Plotting it alongside frame time tells you whether slowdowns are algorithmic or just the canvas being wetter.\\\\n\\\\n**Allocations per frame.** Garbage collection pauses are a major source of jank. Chrome's `performance.memory.usedJSHeapSize` (non-standard, Chromium-only) sampled per frame shows the sawtooth pattern of allocations + GC. If the sawtooth is sharp, you have an allocation hot spot. The kaleidoscope visualization, for instance, allocates a `getBoundingClientRect()` object every frame \\\\u2014 small but visible.\\\\n\\\\n**Long Animation Frames (LoAF).** Newer Performance Observer API exposes frames that blocked the main thread longer than 50ms with attribution to scripts. `new PerformanceObserver(list => ...)` with `entryTypes: ['long-animation-frame']`. Much better than FPS for catching real user-perceptible jank.\\\\n\\\\n**GPU vs CPU time.** Chrome DevTools' Performance tab shows compositor + raster work separately from main-thread JS. For this canvas, JS dominates, but on a transparent body + drawImage upscale, the compositor sometimes shows up as a meaningful chunk.\\\\n\\\\n**Input latency.** Time from `pointermove` event to next paint that reflects it. The pendingPaint queue in this code accumulates per-event coordinates and flushes once per frame, so latency is bounded at ~1 frame + sim cost. Measure it directly by timestamping the event and subtracting at the next `render` call.\\\\n\\\\n**Visual stability (CLS analog).** Less relevant here than for typical pages, but worth knowing: layout shifts in chrome elements could be measured if you cared.\\\\n\\\\nFor this specific tool the highest-value additions would be **(1) p99 frame time**, **(2) per-phase ms breakdown**, and **(3) active cell count**. Together they tell you not just \\\\\\\"is it slow\\\\\\\" but \\\\\\\"what is slow and why.\\\\\\\" A small overlay readout would be cheap \\\\u2014 maybe 15 lines of code, gated behind a `?perf=1` URL flag so it doesn't bother regular users.\\\\n\\\\nWant me to add that as an option in v0.19?\\\",\\n    \\\"citations\\\": []\\n  }\\n]\",\"stderr\":\"\"}"
