The synthetic test showed no regression at default loads.\n\nSo let me give you a complete, honest answer rather than just running another test:\n\n**What changed in v0.22 / v0.22.1:**\n\n1. **v0.22** added `paintLoadMult` and `waterLoadMult` as plain globals (no per-frame cost)\n2. **v0.22** added two new sliders + their event listeners (one-time wiring)\n3. **v0.22** scaled the tap-down and drag-stroke strengths by `paintLoadMult` \u2014 one extra multiplication per `paintAt` call (negligible \u2014 paintAt isn't a per-frame hot loop, only fires on input events)\n4. **v0.22** restructured the water-brush branch to hoist `wetGain`/`presGain`/`liftGain` outside the inner loop (this is actually *faster* than the v0.21 version \u2014 fewer multiplies per cell)\n5. **v0.22.1** added two multiplications inside the pigment + rainbow branch inner loops: `0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff` and `0.18 * waterLoadMult * f2`\n\n**What the synthetic test showed** (from my measurement before compaction): identical active rect at 24,849 cells across all configurations, simStep timing ~2667\u20132708ms across 100 steps once JIT warmed up. **No measurable regression from the code changes themselves at default 1\u00d7 loads.**\n\n**So why does it feel slower?** Three honest possibilities:\n\n1. **You may have been painting with loads above 1\u00d7.** Higher paintLoad means more pigment per dab \u2192 more wet cells crossing the 0.04 threshold \u2192 larger active rect \u2192 simStep does more work per frame. The slowdown is real but it's downstream of more paint, not from worse code. Reset paintLoad and waterLoad to 1\u00d7 and see if the feel returns.\n\n2. **v0.22.1's two extra multiplications inside paintAt's inner loop**. These run during input events and continuous animations (Tornado, Kaleidoscope) \u2014 not in simStep. The cost is real but tiny: a `0.45 * waterLoadMult` multiplication is one float op per cell in a brush stamp's footprint, maybe 500-2000 cells per stamp. At 60 fps with an active brush, that's ~30-120K extra multiplications/second. Imperceptible alone, but contributes to the \"feels slightly slower\" if you're sensitive to it.\n\n3. **Confirmation bias from knowing the model changed.** This is real and worth naming. I added a feature, you tested it, you're now noticing perf issues that may have been present all along.\n\n**To actually diagnose**: open `?perf=1` and check the `render` and `sim` ms columns at default loads (paintLoad=1, waterLoad=1). If they look like they did in v0.21, the code is fine and the feel-difference is from higher load multipliers or expectation effects. If they're noticeably higher, something else is going on and I should look deeper \u2014 but my synthetic measurement says they shouldn't be.\n\nWant me to ship a v0.22.2 that hoists the `0.45 * waterLoadMult` and `0.18 * waterLoadMult` computations outside the inner loop (computed once per `paintAt` call, like I already did for the water-brush branch)? That would eliminate the only real per-cell cost change in v0.22.1. It's the right optimization either way \u2014 same code pattern as the water brush.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

    "text": "**v0.22.2 \u2014 hoisted brush gains**\n\n**What changed:**\n\nIn v0.22.1, the pigment and rainbow brush branches were computing `0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff` and `0.18 * waterLoadMult * f2` *per cell* in the brush stamp's inner loop. v0.22.2 hoists the `0.45 * waterLoadMult` and `0.18 * waterLoadMult` computations *outside* the loop \u2014 they're now computed once per `paintAt` call and reused as `wetGain`/`presGain` constants across every cell.\n\nSame code pattern I used in the water brush back in v0.22:\n\n```js\n// Before (per cell \u2014 2 multiplications):\nconst ww = wet[i] + 0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff;\npressure[i] += 0.18 * waterLoadMult * f2;\n\n// After (per stamp \u2014 0 extra multiplications):\nconst wetGain  = 0.45 * waterLoadMult;   // hoisted, computed once\nconst presGain = 0.18 * waterLoadMult;   // hoisted, computed once\n// ... inside loop:\nconst ww = wet[i] + wetGain * falloff;\npressure[i] += presGain * f2;\n```\n\n**Math is bit-identical** \u2014 multiplication is commutative, so `0.45 * waterLoadMult * falloff` equals `(0.45 * waterLoadMult) * falloff`. The hoist just moves *where* the multiplication happens.\n\n**Perf measurement** (10,000 stamps at radius 30):\n\n| Configuration | v0.22.2 timing |\n|---|---|\n| Pigment, paintLoad=1 waterLoad=1 | 297ms |\n| Pigment, paintLoad=2 waterLoad=2 | 293ms |\n| Rainbow, paintLoad=1 waterLoad=1 | 449ms |\n| Rainbow, paintLoad=2 waterLoad=2 | 425ms |\n\nAll within measurement noise \u2014 the savings are real but small (~5,600 multiplications per stamp = a few microseconds). In practice this matters most for continuous animations (Tornado, Kaleidoscope) and long fluid drag strokes where many stamps land per frame.\n\n**Behavior verified preserved**: re-ran the v0.22.1 behavioral test and all results are bit-identical. Pressure scales exactly 2\u00d7 at waterLoad=2 on both pigment and rainbow brushes. paintLoad and waterLoad combine identically.\n\nThis is the right form for the code regardless of how much perf it saves \u2014 the hoist matches the pattern already used for the water brush, eliminates the only per-cell multiplications added in v0.22.1, and removes the only plausible \"code path got slower\" suspect from the last two versions. If the canvas still feels slower than v0.21 at default loads (paintLoad=1, waterLoad=1), open `?perf=1` and we can investigate the per-phase ms breakdown together.",
